content.text: Tool calling (also called function calling) lets the model request execution of code you define. The model emits a structured
describing which function to invoke and with what arguments; the SDK validates the arguments against your Zod schema, calls your
function, injects the result back into the model context, and the model resumes generating text with that information available.
helper standardises this pattern across all providers — OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, and others — without any provider-specific code in your application.

attrs.alt: AI SDK tool calling lifecycle: prompt → model emits tool_call → SDK calls execute() → result injected → model generates final text

, meaning the model gets one opportunity to call tools before the result is returned — even if there are tool calls left to fulfill.
controls whether and how the model uses tools. The default
Model decides whether to call a tool (default)
Model must call at least one tool before generating text
Model must not call any tool — generates text only
Force a specific named tool — every call uses it
without automatic execution. This is the pattern for human-approval workflows, where a user must review and confirm an action before it runs:
with deferred execution to guarantee that the model always proposes an action. Without it, the model might generate a text response instead of a tool call.
The quality of the Zod schema directly affects how reliably the model populates arguments. Keep schemas flat and add descriptions to every field:
on every parameter — the description becomes the model's only guide for how to populate the field.
Prefer flat schemas over deeply nested objects. Models handle nesting inconsistently, especially at large depths.
for categorical fields — the model can only choose a listed value, eliminating misspellings.
Split tools that do multiple things. A
tool is harder for the model to call correctly than separate
When you provide multiple tools, the model can call several of them in a single step if the answers are independent. The SDK executes all parallel calls concurrently:
lists every tool call in that step. A single step may contain multiple parallel calls; the SDK waits for all of them before feeding the results back to the model.
. Tool call and result events appear in the
Tool execution happens synchronously between text chunks. The stream pauses while
runs, then continues with the model's response to the tool result. For long-running tool calls, stream a placeholder message to the UI while waiting.
Models occasionally format arguments differently than expected — for example, passing a number as a string. Zod's coercion utilities handle these cases without requiring special tool logic:
constraints to catch out-of-range values before they reach your backend.
Coercion is especially useful for models that do not yet have native structured-output enforcement. It makes tools more robust without adding manual parsing code.
lets you simulate tool call responses in unit tests. Provide the
to exercise your tool dispatch and argument parsing code without making real API calls:
Use the mock to test that your
function handles the arguments correctly, that coercion works, and that error cases (wrong types, missing fields) are handled gracefully.
The two approaches solve related but distinct problems. Use
for structured data extraction with no side effects; use tool calling when you need to trigger actions and feed results back to the model:
Extract structured data from text — classification, entity extraction, form filling. No side effects.
Trigger actions (API calls, DB writes) during generation. Return value goes back to the model.
Force a tool call, inspect args, execute manually after user approval (human-in-the-loop).

when tools need to be called sequentially. Without it, the SDK stops after one model call — the model emits a tool call, the SDK executes it, but the model never sees the result and generates a final response.
without providing the target tool. If no tool name is given, the model picks any available tool — which may not be what you intend.
(dates, class instances, circular references). Tool results are serialised to JSON before being injected into the model context — use plain objects.
Deeply nested Zod schemas with optional fields at multiple levels. Models often fail to populate inner optional fields. Flatten the schema and use separate tools for distinct operations.
